note.soap:
S: Patient reports a tick bite with attachment duration of approximately 37 hours. No systemic symptoms such as fever, chills, or malaise reported. Patient notes presence of a bullseye rash at the bite site. Exposure occurred in Georgia. Pregnancy is not possible.

O: Unable to perform physical exam due to phone-only encounter. Patient description of rash consistent with erythema migrans.

A:
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Early localized Lyme disease, suggested by bullseye rash and tick exposure >36 hours in an endemic area.
  - Differential:
    1. Early Lyme disease
    2. Tick-bite hypersensitivity reaction
    3. Cellulitis or local skin infection
    4. Southern tick-associated rash illness (STARI)
  - Red flags considered: No systemic symptoms such as fever, neurological signs, or joint pain reported. No signs of severe allergic reaction.

P:
  - Disposition: Routine follow-up with primary care or infectious disease provider recommended.
  - Plan: Initiate empiric antibiotic therapy for early Lyme disease per guidelines (e.g., doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10-21 days) given presence of erythema migrans and tick exposure >36 hours in an endemic area. Educate patient on signs of progression including fever, worsening rash, joint pain, or neurological symptoms.
  - Safety net: Advise patient to seek urgent care if systemic symptoms develop (fever, severe headache, facial palsy, joint swelling), rash rapidly expands, or if unable to tolerate oral antibiotics.

Evidence (snippets used): No direct evidence snippets retrieved; management based on standard clinical guidelines for Lyme disease diagnosis and treatment in endemic regions.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: CDC: What to Do After a Tick Bite
CDC: Guidance for Clinicians—Caring for Patients after a Tick Bite (PDF)
IDSA/AAN/ACR: 2020 Guidelines for Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Lyme Disease
AAFP: Lyme Disease—Updated Recommendations from IDSA/AAN/ACR (2021)
